text: BATHAMPTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 2 m. NE of Bath. Area 860 acres. Pop. 354.
BATHEALTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. S of Wiveliscombe. Area 740 acres. Pop. 135.

BATHEASTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 21 m. NE of the city of Bath, on the N bank of the Avon. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. 2,191. The line of houses in this p. between it and B., being uninterrupted, may be regarded as a part of that city.
BATHFORD, a parish in Somersetshire, 4 m. ENE of Bath. Area 2,030 acres. Pop. 1,099.

BATHGATE, a parish and town in Linlithgowshire.—The p. has an area of 11,214 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,513; in 1841, 3,928, of whom 2,809 were in the town.—The town is situated on the middle line of road from Edinburgh to Glasgow, 18 m. from the former, and 24 m. from the latter. Its pop. is chiefly supported by weaving cotton-goods, and by the lime, coal, and iron works in the vicinity. A branch rail- BAT way connects B. with the Edinburgh and Glasgow railway.
BATHLEY, a township in the p. of North Muskham, Nottinghamshire. Pop. 252. See also Bale.
BATHMEN, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Overyssel, on the road from Deventer to Holten.
BATHOR (NYIR), a town and district of Hungary, in the circle of Szabolcs, 20 m. NE of Debreczin. Pop. 3,142. It belongs to the Bathory family.
BATHUMI. See BATUM.

BATHURST, a county in New South Wales, stretching along the W bank of the Macquarie, which separates it on the E from the counties of Roxburgh and Westmoreland, and bounded on the S by Georgiana. More definitely, its boundaries on the SE are the river Campbell from Pepper creek to its junction with the Macquarie; the Macquarie to the Currigurra on the E; the Currigurra river, and a line of marked trees to the Molong river, on the N; the Molong, the Panuara range of hills, and the upper part of Limestone creek, on the W; and the Lachlan road on the S. The district is naturally destitute of timber; but the banks of the Macquarie are lined with swamp-oaks, a tree resembling the Scotch fir. A considerable quantity of cheese is made on the large farms; and large herds of black cattle are reared. The plain of B. is about 19 m. in length, and from 6 m. to 8 m. in breadth, and consists of a series of gentle elevations with intervening plains of moderate extent. The view of the vale of the Macquarie from Mount Pleasant, a conical hill of between 300 or 400 ft. in height above the plain, reminded Dr. Lang of the view from Stirling castle in Scotland. The plain of B. is upwards of 2,100 ft. above sealevel, an elevation which compensates for 10 degrees of lat.; and the vegetation is exactly similar to that of Van Diemen's land, 10° further S.—The cap. of the same name is situated on the l. bank of the Macquarie, 140 m. NW of Sydney, with which it is connected by a good road.—It is now a fine town, and reckoned the Montpelier of New South Wales.
BATHURST, or St. Peter's, a port of New Brunswick, on the S side of the bay of Chaleur. It is the harbour of Nipisighit bay, and large ships load with timber here inside of the bar.

BATHURST, an extensive district of Upper Canada, lying on the E or r. bank of the Ottawa, and stretching from the parallel of 44° 30′ to that of 46°. It is watered by the Bonnechere, the Madawaska, and the Mississippi rivers; and its surface is mountainous and sprinkled with lakes. Its principal towns, Lanark, Carleton, and Richmond, lie on its S frontier. Pop. in 1842, 21,086, of whom 11,247 were British.
BATHURST, a town of S Africa, in Albany co., nearly mid-distance between Graham's town and Theopolis.

BATHURST, or St. Mary's, a town of W. Africa, within the mouth of the Gambia, at the E extremity of St. Mary's isle, a long narrow sand-bank, insulated towards the S by a nearly impassable mangrove creek, and on the N side by the main stream of the river, which at this point is nearly 3 m. across. The town is a small but prosperous-looking place, with several excellent stone houses, especially on the wharf. It is 300 m. from Barraconda falls; 180 m. from Calabar; and 175 m. from Macarthy's island. The merchants of B. have from 20 to 24 factories, great and small, in the various towns along the river. Most of these are mere huts, hired from the natives, and converted into temporary storehouses for goods, which are managed by coloured men. The trade is carried on up the river by small craft varying from 5 to 60 tons. On the river face are 3 batteries mounting 8 guns and 3 mortars; and on the side towards the creek are 2 other batteries. On the opposite point of Barra, nearer the mouth of the river, is a quadrangular stone fort with circular bastions. The total pop. of the island of St. Mary's was 3,514 in 1840, of whom 42 were whites, and 3,291 coloured people. Of this pop. 580 were employed in manufactures; 271 in agriculture; and 370 in trade. It has an area of about 5 sq. m. The soil is barely perceptibly elevated above the sea; and the banks are so low, that when the seaward current sets in, and the wind is high, the centre of the island is swamped, and the houses in the upper part of the town are inundated. The whole island, with the exception of about 650 acres, is a sandy waste, with intervals of mangrove-swamp. About 60 acres are under cultivation. The imports into B. from 1836 to 1840 were as follows:- £ £ £ £ £ Total, The exports during the same five years were as follows:- £ £ £ £ £ Total, This trade employed about 1,270 vessels, of an aggregate tonnage of 67,000 tons, and manned by 10,000 hands.

BATHURST (CAPE), a promontory on the Polar coast of America, in N lat. 70° 36', W long. 127° 35'. The coast here consists of precipitous banks, similar in structure to the shale-cliffs at Whitby in Yorkshire, and in many places in a state of ignition. They rise in height from 30 ft. to 600 ft.—Franklin.

BATHURST ISLAND, an island off the NW coast of New Holland, the largest of the group which forms the E shore of Sunday strait. It bears SSW 6 m. from the Macleay isles. A thick growth of red gum and the papyrus clothes part of it. The prevailing rock is composed of sandstone and quartz, and there are appearances of iron upon it. [Stokes.] -Also one of the North Georgian islands, discovered by Parry in 1819-20. Its S shores skirt on the parallel of 75° N; and it lies between Bijam Martin island on the W, and Cornwallis island on the E.— Also a large island off the coast of North Australia, separated from the main by Clarence strait leading into Van Diemen gulf, and from Melville island on the E by a narrow intricate passage called Apsley strait. See article Australia, p. 458.

BATHURST (LAKE), a sheet of water in New South Wales, in the co. of Argyle, 60 m. inland from Jervis bay, the nearest part of the coast. It is from 3 to 5 m. in diam.; and being a mere reservoir formed by the rains from the contiguous mountains, is subject to great increase and decrease. Its waters, however, are pure; and an animal resembling a seal is said to inhabit it. BAT
BATHURST HARBOUR, a long irregular inlet of the sea on the SW coast of Van Diemen's land, in 43° 15' S lat. It forms an eastward prolongation of Port Davey.
BATHWICK, a parish in Somersetshire, now within the parliamentary boundaries of Bath. Area 630 acres. Pop. 4,972.
BATIAN, one of the Philippine group, off the S coast of Mindanao, in N lat. 7º 12', E long. 122° 50'.
BATIE-DIVISIN (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Isere, cant. of Saint-Geoirs. Pop. 1,234.
BATIE-MONTGASCON (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Isere, cant. of Pont-de-Beauvoisin. Pop. 1,311.
BATIE-NEUVE (LA), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, arrond. of Gap.—The cant. comprises 8 com. The com., 6 m. E of Gap, at the source of the Laie, has a pop.
BATIGNANO, a village of Tuscany, NE of Gros-
BATILLI, a commune of France, in the dep. of
BATINDA, a district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Ajmeer, 75 m. SW of Ludiana. The surrounding country, a rich pasture district, is called the Lacky jungle, and is celebrated for its breed of horses.

BATISCAN, a district of Lower Canada, in Chamriver of the same name. It is 60 m. long, by 6 m. in interior from Quebec, and flows into the St. Lawwide at its mouth; but the entrance is obstructed by a sand-bank, and the navigation is interrupted by
BATIYATZ, a small town of Servia, 30 m. E of Semendria.
BATKALUISH, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Astrakhan, 80 m. SW of Astrakhan, in N lat. 45° 35'.
BATKAKLUM, a sandy desert of Bokhara, on the S slope of the Kukertli mountain.

BATLEY, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. from Wakefield. Area 6,390 acres. Pop. 14,278, of whom 7,076 were in the township of the same name. BATN-EL-HAJAR [i. e. 'the Womb of Rocks'], a rocky tract of Nubia, extending on both sides of the Nile, between the Wadi Halfah on the N, and Sukkot on the S, and embracing the most rocky and broken part of the bed of the Nile. The geological formation is primitive. The prevailing rock here is greenstone and greywacke. It is sometimes called Dar-el-Hajar, or 'the mansion of Rocks.' The navigation of the river was quite interrupted here by rocks and rapids until Mahommed Ali's engineers cleared away some of the obstructions. The inhabitants are few in number; they are of a dark brown colour, with fine features, and well-made. They cultivate a few narrow spots which occur at intervals on the side of the river.—Burckhardt.
BATNA, a district of Oman, in Arabia, extending in a narrow strip along the coast from Sib to Korfakan.
BATNEER. See BHUTNIR.
BATO, one of the Ladrone islands, in N lat. 12° 20', E long. 142° 10'.

BATON ROUGE (East), a parish in the state of Louisiana, U. S., on the E bank of the Mississippi. It is the first elevated land seen in ascending that river. Its productions are cotton, Indian corn, and sugar. Pop. 8,138. Its cap., of the same name, is 30 m. above Donaldsonville, and 150 m. below Natchez. Pop. 2,269.
BATON ROUGE (West), a parish on the W side of the Mississippi, opposite the above. Its surface is nearly a dead level. Its principal production is cotton. Pop. 4,638.
BATONYA, a town of Hungary, on the l. bank of a bifurcation of the Maros, 6 m. NW of Tornya.

BATOPILAS (San Pedro de), a town of Mexico, in the division of New Biscay, 150 m. SW of Chihuahua. There were mines of silver in the neighbourhood of this town, in which the ore is said to have been so rich as to admit of the metal being actually cut out with a chisel.
BATOS, a town of Austria, in Transylvania, in the co. of Kotosch, 9 m. N by W of Szasz Regen, on the road to Bistritz.
BATORZ, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Lublin, circle of Zamosc. Pop. 580.
BATOUM. See BATUM.
BATRES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 20 m. NNE of Toledo, near the Guadarrama.

BATRUN, or Bathron, a town of Syria, 20 m. S of Tripoli, on the coast. Its harbour is now filled up with mud and sand. Pop. about 1,500, chiefly Maronites. Tobacco is cultivated in the neighbourhood; and silk, cotton, and potash are exported.
BATS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Geaune. Pop. 402.
BATSCHIAN. See BACHIAN.
BATSFALU, a Wallachian village of Austria, in Siebenburgen, in the Kronstadt district. Pop. 1,332.
BATSFORD, a parish of Gloucestershire, 2 m. NW of Moreton. Area 980 acres. Pop. 79.

BATTA, or BATAK, a district extending along the W coast of the island of Sumatra, from the equator to about the parallel of 2° 30' N, between the Sinkel river, which divides it from Acheen, on the NW, and the Tabuyong river on the SE, which divides it from Menancabo. It is traversed by several rivers which descend from the Deirah and Papa mountains, and overflow the low grounds during the rainy season; and an extensive lake, called Toba, is said to exist in the interior, to the NE of Tappanooly. The productions of the B. territory are gold, sulphur, benzoin, camphor, cassia, cotton, and indigo. It is divided into numerous petty and apparently independent rajaships, who are, however, said to pay a certain ecclesiastical deference to a chief who holds sway near the NW extremity of Lake Toba; and to profess a species of political fealty to the sultan of Menancabo. The total pop. of the B. territory has been estimated by Messrs. Burton and Ward, who visited this country in 1824, so high as 1,500,000; but there are no absolute data for this.

BATTAS, the native pop. of the above territory, in the island of Sumatra. They are a race of lower stature and fairer complexion than the Malays. They are partial to the use of mixed colours in their dress; and the young women ornament themselves with ear-rings and armlets of gold. Polygamy is practised by them in its utmost latitude; but, should a man have as many as six wives, each has her own fire-place, though all sleep in the same apartment, there being no partitions in the houses. The Battas are Mahommedans; but they believe in intermediate deities, whose names seem to be of Hindu origin; and they have a confused idea of the immortality of the soul. Their funerals are performed with much ceremony, should the deceased be a person of consequence. The body is kept a long time until a great concourse be assembled, and deposition in the grave is accompanied with the sacrifice of animals, which is sometimes continued during a whole year afterwards. The B. language bears a great resemblance to that of the Malays. Their alphabet consists of nineteen letters; and they write from the bottom to the top, in a way the reverse of the Chinese method. The more potent rajahs assume power over the lives of their subjects, and derive their revenues from the fines of cattle adjudged in criminal proceedings, and the produce of the camphor and benzoin trees within their territories. In the administration of the laws, the lives of crimi- BAT their children may be voluntarily sold by their husbands. Rice and sweet potatoes, with an unusual proportion of salt, form the principal food of the B. They are, however, said to be fond of horse-flesh, and to fatten these animals for food. It has been reported that they are cannibals; but Marsden affirms that they eat human flesh rather as a kind of ceremonial, and as a declaration of the ignominy of the person devoured. The objects of these savage repasts are prisoners of war, and offenders condemned for certain capital crimes, especially adultery. Dr. Leyden represents them as regarding it as an act of pious duty to eat their aged or infirm relatives at a solemn banquet to which they are invited by the victim himself! These people are frequently engaged in wars, which are chiefly carried on in detail, and they generally endeavour to destroy their enemies by stealth. Their villages are fortified with large ramparts of earth, and an impenetrable hedge of prickly bamboo, as also a palisade and ditch; at each corner is a tall tree as a watch-tower. The women fabricate and dye cloth; and the men work iron, and manufacture a coarse gunpowder. The B. have been known as a nation for above four centuries.— Marsden.—Leyden.—Asiatic Society's Transactions, vol. i.—Voyage of Potomac.
BATTA, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Tolna, on the r. bank of the Danube, 4 m. SSE of Battaszek, in N lat. 46° 8'.
BATTA (Gungo), a district of the kingdom of Congo, in Lower Guinea, to the E of San Salvador.

BATTAGLIA, a village of Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, on the canal of Monselice, 10 m. SSW of Padua. Pop. 3,000. There are hot baths here of a temp. of 135°, which are much frequented. In the neighbourhood is the antique castle of Catajo, now the property of the duke of Modena.—Also a v. of Naples, in Principato Citra, in the district of Sala. Pop. 1,218.
BATTAHATCHEE, a river of North America, which rises in the state of Alabama, and after a course of 70 m. enters the Tombigbee in Monroe co., Missouri.
BATTALAH, or VATALA, a town of Lahore, 25 m. NE of Amritsir, in N lat. 31° 47', in an extensive plain, surrounded with groves of mangoes and other fruit trees.
BATTALEN, a small island off the W coast of Norway, in N lat. 61° 35'.
BATTAM, an island on the S side of the straits of Singapore, in N lat. 1° 5', E long. 104° 10', to the W of Bintang.

BATTANG-MORING, a hamlet near to the v. of Gummi, in the Malay peninsula, in the Muar territory. There is a gold-mine here which is wrought by Chinese, but is said to be nearly exhausted. The metal is said to be remarkably pure.

BATTECOLLAH, or BATUCALA, a port of Hindostan, in the prov. of Canara, in N lat. 13° 55', E long. 74° 36'. Pop. 1,200. It is finely situated on the N bank of the Scandaholaya, which winds through a fertile valley enclosed by richly cultivated hills.
BATTEL. See BATTLE.
BATTELAU, a town of Austria, in Moravia, in the circle and 12 m. SW of Iglau. Pop. 1,500.
BATTELMA, a village of Syria, in the pash, of Aleppo, 6 m. SW of Antakia, near the l. bank of the Assi. It is surrounded by ruins supposed to be those of the ancient Daphne.
BATTENBERG, a village of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the circle of the same name, on the Eder, 40 m. N of Giessen. Pop. 954.
BATTENFELD, a village adjoining the above. Pop. 561.
BATTENHEIM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Habsheim. Pop. 1,092.
BATTENKILL, a river of North America, which rises in the state of Vermont, and after a course of 50 m., enters the Hudson in Washington co., in the state of New York, 13 m. below Fort Miller.

BATTERSEA, a parish in Surrey, 4 m. SW of London, on the S bank of the Thames, and intersected by the London and Southampton railroad. Area 3,020 acres. Pop. in 1801, 3,365; in 1841, nals are redeemable in almost all cases; although women and | 6,887. A great part of the surface of this p. is laid out in market-gardens, from which the metropolis obtains a large supply of fruit and vegetables. A training-school for schoolmasters has recently been established here under the direction of the Council of Education.
BATTESFORD, a parish in Suffolk, 2 m. W of Needham. Area 1,340 acres. Pop. 520.

BATTLE, a parish and village in Sussex, 7 m. NW of Hastings. Area of p., 7,880 acres. Pop. 3,039. The v., which is situated in a fine valley encircled on three sides by wooded hills, consists principally of a single street running up a rising ground, at a little distance from the upper termination of which stands the celebrated abbey of Battle, its grand entrance facing the street. The town is famous for the manufacture of gunpowder, for which there are several mills. This place was formerly called St. Maryin-the-Wood, and took its present appellation from the celebrated battle between William, duke of Normandy, and Harold, king of England, fought on the 14th of October, 1066, in which the latter lost his crown and life. This is usually called the battle of Hastings, though in fact it took place at a considerable distance to the northward of that town, on a heath near the village of Epiton. After the battle, William caused his pavilion to be pitched among the corpses, and supped with his barons on the field. The abbey of B. was most richly endowed: and all the land, for one league round about, was annexed to its franchise. The abbot was freed from the authority of the metropolitan of Canterbury, and invested with archiepiscopal jurisdiction. The high altar was erected on the very spot where Harold's standard had waved; and the roll, deposited in the archives of the monastery, recorded the names of those who had fought with the Conqueror, and amongst whom the lands of broad England were divided. The extent of the ruins is still sufficient to show the magnificence of the original structure, which must have been nearly a mile in circuit.
BATTLE, a parish and v. in Breconshire, 3 m. NE of Brecknock. Pop. 176.
BATTLE-BRIDGE, a hamlet in the parish of Tumna, co. Roscommon, at the W end of a bridge which crosses the Shannon, and leads to the v. of Leitrim. Pop. 147.

BATTLE-CREEK, a township in Calhoun co., in the state of Michigan, U.S., 12 m. NW of Marshall, at the junction of a stream of the same name, 40 m. in length, with the Kabamazoo. Pop. 993.—Also a town in Marion co., Tennessee.

BATTLEFIELD, a parish in the co. of Salop, 4 m. NE of Shrewsbury. Area 850 acres. Pop. 64. The name is derived from a battle which was fought here on the 21st of July, 1403, between Henry IV., and Percy, earl of Northumberland, aided by the earl of Douglass and a body of Scots. The struggle was doubtful for the space of three hours; but on the death of Hotspur, terminated in favour of the royalists.
BATTLE-ISLANDS, a group forming the S point of St. Lewis sound, on the coast of Labrador, in N lat. 52° 15′ 44".
BATTO, an islet off the NW coast of Timor, in S lat. 9°.
BATTO-BOOTIE, a town on the SE coast of the island of Timor, in S lat. 10° 40'.
BATTULAKI, a port at the N extremity of the island of Magindanao, in N lat. 5° 42', E long. 125°.

BATU, or MINTAO PULO, an island off the W coast of the island of Sumatra, lying NW and SE between 0° 5' and 0° 41' S lat., and in E long. 98° 15'. It is about 48 m. in length, and 16 m. in breadth, and is moderately elevated. It is well-wooded, and produces cocoa-nuts, oil, sago, hogs, poultry, and trepang. Numerous islets fringe its coasts. BAT

BATUANAS, a tribe of Bechuanas in S Africa, whose territory, a beautiful and fertile country, lies between the 19th and 20th parallels of S lat., 556 m. NNW of Kolobong, around a great inland lake, the existence of which was first ascertained in July, 1849. The B. are a numerous tribe. Their principal town is within 6 m. of the lake, which is called Noka-ca-Nama, or Ngama. The missionaries, in their first report of it, say: "We cannot tell how broad it may be, for we could not see a horizon, except one of water, on the S and W. We traversed through much desert country, and were looking for the lake for 200 m. before we came to it. We traversed about 200 m. along the banks of a large river which runs SSE, a beautiful stream, in some parts very like the Clyde, but frequently broader. The water was rising, and seems to come from the N, from melted snows, it is so clear and soft. Two large rivers run into the lake, both from the N."

BATU-BARRA, a river of Sumatra, flowing into the straits of Malacca, in N lat. 3° 13'. A small port of the same name stands at its embouchure, whence salted fish, rattans, and silks are exported, and an extensive coasting-trade is maintained.
BATU-BUKET, a town on the E coast of Sumatra, in the Siak territory, in N lat. 1° 25'.

BATUECAS (LES), a secluded valley or Alpine gorge of Spain, in the prov. of Leon, about 3 m. in length, and 2 m. wide, in which a celebrated convent of Carmelites was founded in 1599. The soil is rocky and arid; but the monks have succeeded in adorning their retreat with fine cedar and cypress trees.
BATU-MANDAWI, a cluster of islets lying off the S coast of Borneo, opposite the mouth of the Great Borneo river, in S lat. 3° 30'.

BATUM, or BATUMI, a seaport of Asiatic Turkey, in the prov. of Guria, situated to the NE of the mouth of the Jorak or Chorukh, in N lat. 41° 40', E long. 41° 35′ 25", at the foot of a high ridge of mountains. Its harbour is good, and affords a tolerably spacious and safe anchorage in from 15 to 18 fath.; but the station is unhealthy in the summer months. The town has a pop. of about 2,000, whose scattered habitations make it look more like a cluster of hamlets than a town. There is a British vice-consul here. The neighbouring country produces wheat, rice, and olives; and the chief exports are maize, rice, wax, and honey. It is understood that Russia is desirous of obtaining the cession of this the last port of Turkey in the Black sea.—Mahlmann's Monatsberichte, 1845. -Geog. Journ., vol. vi.
BATURIM, a town of Russia, in the Ukraine, on the Sem river, 80 m. ESE of Czernigov.

BATURITE', a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceara, on the Serra Baturite, a part of the Cordillera de Borborema. It was originally a settlement of Cairiris Indians. The pop. of the town and district is 2,200.

BATZ, an island of France, in the dep. of Finistere, cant. of Saint-Pol-de-Leon, about half-a-mile from the coast. Pop. 1,032. It is <math>1\frac{1}{2}</math> m. in length, by \( \frac{3}{4} \) m. in breadth.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Croisic, 3 m. S of Guerande. Pop. 3,643. Upwards of 17,000,000 kilog, of salt is annually manufactured here from salines in the neighbourhood.
BATZA, an island in the Vistula, opposite Thorn, in Western Prussia.
BAUBAN, a town on the N coast of the island of Luzon, in the Philippine group, in N lat. 18° 44'.

BAUBELTHOUAP, the largest of the group of the Pelew islands. It is about 120 m. in length. Lat. of E end, 7°41', E long. 134° 55'. Its pop. was estimated by Holden and his companions—who were wrecked upon it in 1832-at about 2,000. The men go entirely naked, and always armed with a spear and tomahawk. They are fantastically tattooed on different parts of their bodies; and their hair, which is coarse and black, is allowed to hang loosely over their shoulders. The women wear a sort of apron, fastened to the waist by a curiously wrought girdle, both being manufactured from the bark of a tree. Their complexion is a light copper; their eyes have a singularly reddish colour. Their noses are flat, but not so much so as those of the African Negroes .- Holden's Narrative, Boston, U. S., 1836.

BAUCHERVILLE, or BOUCHERVILLE, a village and seigniory of Lower Canada, on the S bank of the St. Lawrence, 10 m. NE of Montreal, in Chambly co. The v. contains about 100 houses. Pop. of the parish, 2,800. The agricultural productions are wheat, oats, and maple sugar.
BAUD, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, arrond. of Pontivy. The cant. comprises 5 com. The com. is 14 m. S of Pontivy. Pop. 5,120.
BAUDEL (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, cant. of La Guerche-sur-l'Aubois, 20 m. SSW of Bourges. Pop. 837. There are ironforges here.
BAUDET, a river of Lower Canada, which traverses the township of Lancaster, and falls into Lake St. Francis.
BAUDMANNSDORF, a village of Prussia, in Silesia, in the circle of Leignitz, near Goldberg- Hainau.
BAUDO, a town of Cundinamarca, in the prov. of Choco, on the r. bank of a small river of the same name, which falls into the Pacific at Point Baudo, in N lat. 5° 20'.
BAUEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Uri, 4 m. from Altdorf, on the lake of the Four Cantons. Pop. 104.
BAUERWITZ, a village of Prussia, in Silesia, 35 m. S of Oppeln. Pop. in 1837, 2,150.
BAUG. See BAG.

BAUGE', an arrondissement, canton, and commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire .- Area of arrond. 139,002 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 81,025; in 1846, 80,341. It comprises the cant. of B., Beaufort, Durtal, Longue, Noyant, and Seiches. -The cant. comprises 17 com., and had a pop. in 1831 of 17,821.—The com. and v. are 20 m. ENE of Angers, on the Couanon, which is here crossed by a fine bridge. Pop. in 1836, 3,400; in 1846, 3,220. The duke of Clarence, at the head of an English force, was defeated and killed here, in 1421, by Lafayette, the general of Charles VII.
BAUGE-LE-VIEIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, a little SW of Baugé. Pop. 1,926.
BAUGHURST, a parish in Hampshire, 4 m. E of Kingsclere. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 528.
BAUGHMAN, a township in Wayne co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., on Newman's creek. Pop. 1,741.
BAUGREE, or BAGHARI, a jungly pergunnah in Bengal, in the district of Midnapur, about 60 m. from Calcutta. It was so recently as 1816 the retreat of a ferocious band of marauders called choars.
BAUGY, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, arrond. of Bourges. The cant. comprises 16 com. The com., 15 m. E of Bourges, has a pop. of 887.
BAUITY, a village in the Libyan desert, near the Egyptian frontier, SE of Siwah.

BAULA, a remarkable conical mountain in Iceland, in the Westfirdinga flordung, in N lat. 64° 50', W long. 21° 25'. It is at least 3,000 ft. in height, and is visible from many parts of the island. It forms the S boundary of a vast oval, or crater, containing several other cones of a smaller size. Its lower regions are covered with white coloured basaltes, forming prisms from 3 to 7 ft. in length, by 5 or 9 in. in diam., and for the most part five or seven sided.—Henderson.
BAULADU, a village of Sardinia, on the road from Milis to Palio. Pop. 500. BAU
BAULE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lorret, cant. of Beaugency, near the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 2,095. The chief products of the district are wine, saffron, and beet-root sugar.
BAULEAH, or Balia, a town of Bengal, in the district of Rajshahi, in N lat. 24° 33', on the N side of the Padha or Great Ganges.

BAUK-BALUN, a river of Tartary, in Kokan, in the steppes of the Kirghiz Kassaks, which rises near Mount Khaltai, on the W frontier of Omsk, and flows in a W by N, bending round to W by S, course of 150 or 200 m. into a salt lake on the meridian of 69°.
BAULKH. See BALKH.
BAULKING, a hamlet and chapelry in the p. of Uffington, Berks, 3 m. SE of Farringdon. Pop. 193.
BAULON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Guicher, 14 m. SW of Rennes. Pop. 1,416.
BAUMA, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 17 m. E of Zurich, on the Toss. Pop. of p. 3,217; of v. 1,550. A considerable quantity of cotton-goods is woven in this district.
BAUMBER, or BAMBURGH, a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. NW of Horncastle. Area 3,200 acres. Pop. 371.
BAUME, or BEAUME, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, at the foot of the Aiguille de Beaume, NW of Lausanne. Pop. 680.
BAUME (SAINTE), a mountain of France, in the dep. of Var, 16 m. WSW of Brignoles. Alt. 860 metres, or 2,821 ft.

BAUME-LES-DAMES, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Doubs.—The arrond., comprising 7 cant., has an area of 147,076 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 67,888; in 1846, 67,826. The cant. are B., Clerval, Isle-sur-le-Donbs, Pierre-Fontaine-les-Varans, Rougemont, Roulans, and Vercel.—The cant., comprising 31 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 9,943.—The town, the chief place in the arrond., is 19 m. NE of Besançon, at an alt. of 943 ft. above sea-level. It is a place of considerable antiquity, and has a considerable trade in cattle, and kirschwasser or cherry-brandy. There are paper and tan works in the town, and gypsum is dug in the neighbourhood.
BAUMIAN. See Bamian.
BAUMERSDORF, a village of Austria, in the Lower Mannhartsberg circle, SE of Deutsch-Wagram.
BAUMGARTEN, a village of Lower Austria, on the Wien, near Vienna. Pop. 357. Prince Esterhazy has a castle here.
BAUMGARTENBERG, a village of Austria, in the circle of Muhl, SW of Grein.
BAUMHOLDER, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhone, circle of Treves, 12 m. NE of Saint- Wendel. Pop. 1,000. Agates are extensively procured and polished here.
BAUMKRUG, a village of Prussia, in the gov. of Königsberg, E of Labiau.

BAUNACH, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Unter-Franken-Aschaffenburg, 6 m. N of Bamberg, at the confluence of the Baunach and the Lauter. Pop. 1,000.—Also a river of Bavaria, which rises in the Hassberg, and flows into the Main, below the Itz.
BAUNAN, a district of W Africa, reported to be 15 days journey S of Kong. The Mandingo merchants trade with this territory, exchanging salt and cloth for colat nuts and gold.—Caillie.
BAUNE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Seiches. Pop. 1,197.

BAUNEI, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. of Lanusei, 60 m. NNE of Cagliari, near Cape Monte- Santo on the E coast. Pop. 1,347. Valery lauds the primitive manners of the pop. of this district and village; and describes a peculiar species of bread which is manufactured here from acorns.
BAUNSKEHA, a village in the p. of Jerpoint- West, co. Kilkenny. Pop. 168.
BAUNDOOGUR. See BANDUGUR.
BAUNTON, a parish in Gloucestershire, 2 m. N of Cirencester. Area 1,310 acres. Pop. 187.

BAURES, or BAURE, a large river of Upper Peru, which rises in Lake Guazumiri, under the parallel of 15° 25', and flows N by W to San Simao, whence it bends NW by N, and receives in succession the Rio San Joaquim, and the Rio Iraibi, on its l. bank; it then flows N by W to San Miguel, whence it bends NW, and joins the Guapore on the l. bank opposite Leonit, after a course of 350 m.
BAURI, or BARI, a district of Lahore, in India, called Mangha by the Sikhs. It contains Lahore, the ancient cap. of this part of Hindostan, and Amritsir.
BAURROE, a village in the p. of Feakle, co. Clare. Pop. 121.
BAUSCHLOT, a town of Baden, in the Middle Rhine circle, SE of Carlsruhe. Pop. 804.
BAUSKE, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Courland, near the source of the Aa. Pop. 1,200.
BAUSSAINE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Tinteniac. Pop. 1,223.
BAUTERSEIN, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of S Brabant, SE of Lowen. Pop. 507.

BAUTSCH, a town of Austria, in Moravia, in the circle of Prerauer. Pop. 2,700. BAUTZEN, BAUZEN, or BUDESSIN, a town of Saxony, in Upper Lusatia, on a rocky eminence overhanging the river Spree, in N lat. 51° 11', 30 m. E by N of Dresden. Its streets are straight and well-built, and the public buildings are numerous. Its manufactures consist of linen and cotton stuffs, hosiery, hats, leather, and paper. Pop. 8,387, principally Lutherans. The German part of the pop., both Catholic and Protestant, worship together in the cathedral; the former are in possession of the third part of it, including the high altar, which is sufficiently large for the small Catholic congregation; the nave serves the Lutheran community as their parish church, and the mutual spirit of toleration in both parties has, in recent times, prevented trouble from such an arrangement. Here was fought, on the 20th and 21st of May, 1813, the second great battle in the campaign of the Prussians and Russians against the French. The allies had been compelled, after the battle of Lützen (May 2, 1813), to retreat to the right bank of the Elbe; and prepared themselves near B. for a new engagement. Although the army of Napoleon was far superior in number, being strengthened by reinforcements from France, Italy, and the troops of the confederation of the Rhine, so as to amount to about 148,000 men, yet the allies determined to risk a battle, that Prussia might gain time for its levies in Silesia, and Napoleon's advance be checked as much as possible. It was also desirable that the wavering cabinet of Austria should be convinced that the army was able to make a stand against the enemy, and that the courage of the new Prussian recruits should not be damped by continual retreat. On the morning of May 20th, Napoleon disclosed his plan of attack. In the evening, the French had gained the town of B. On the 21st, the fight continued until 4 o'clock in the afternoon, when the allies resolved on a retreat, which was performed in such order, that Napoleon was not able to gain any immediate advantage from his victory. The field of battle was covered with the dead, and was lighted by 30 burning villages. The French loss was about 8,000 men killed, and 18,000 erection of a monument upon Mount Cenis, as a token of his | 5. gratitude towards the French and the Italian troops. The rear 6. of the allies repulsed two serious attacks, and, contrary to the expectations of Napoleon, they marched to the entrenched camp 7. of Pülzen. But Lauriston occupied Breslau. The position of the 8. allies, threatening the right wing of the French army, the great loss which the French had suffered, and the detached corps, which cut off Napoleon's communication with Saxony, induced him to accede to a suspension of arms on the 4th of June, near the city of Jauer.
BAUVECHAIN-TOURRINES, a commune of | mountainous: it there borders on Salzburg, Tyrol, BAV Belgium, in Brabant, in the arrond. of Louvain. Pop. 1,852.
BAUVIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Seclin. Pop. 970.
BAUWELZ, a commune of Belgium, in Hainault, 35 m. SW of Charleroi. Pop. 680.

BAUX (ISLE), an island supposed to be about 15 leagues in circuit, in N lat. 8° 54', W long. 140° 5'. It was so named by Marchaud, in 1791, in honour of his mercantile patrons; but it is the SIR HENRY MARTIN'S ISLAND of preceding English navigators. There is an excellent harbour on its S coast.
BAUX (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. of Saint-Remy. Pop. 510.
BAUX-DE-BRETEUIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Breteuil. Pop. 1,621.
BAUZAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Loire, cant. of Monistrol. Pop. 2,597.
BAUZI, a town of Turkey, in Albania, on the l. bank of the Dresnitza, 20 m. W of Staria.
BAUZILLE-DE-PUTOIS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Herault, 20 m. NNW of Montpellier, on the Herault. Pop. 1,622.

BAVARIA. In German, Baiern,—in French, Baviere,—a German state, the thirteenth in the list of European states with regard to extent and population. The original country of the Bavarian monarchy was the duchy of Bavaria, which took its name from its first inhabitants, the Boii, or Bojoarii. This division of Germany, -successively a circle, a duchy, and an electorate, - prospered and grew into a kingdom under the fostering care of Napoleon, who enlarged its territories at the expense of Austria and Prussia, and the neighbouring petty princes of the Germanic body, especially those of the spiritual order. It is more than one-half greater in extent now than it was in 1777. Two districts of unequal size, and of which the contiguity is interrupted by the districts of Baden and Hesse-Darmstadt, form this state. The larger of these lies in the heart of Germany, to the E of the Rhine; the smaller is on the left or W bank of the Rhine. The former,—generally called the country of the Danube and the Main, -is bounded on the N by the duchy of Saxony, and the principality of Reuss; on the NE by the kingdom of Saxony; on the E and S by the Austrian dominions and Switzerland; and on the W by Wirtemberg, Baden, and Hesse-Darmstadt. The other division, or the territory of the Rhine, is bounded on the N by the Prussian prov. of the Lower Rhine, and Hesse-Darmstadt; on the E by the Rhine, which separates it from the grand-duchy of Baden; on the S by France; and on the W by the Prussian prov. of the Lower Rhine and Hesse-Hamburg.—The total superficial extent of B., according to Hassel, is 1,454, according to Stein 1,338, according to Rudhart 1,382; and according to Berghaus, 1,394 German sq. m. Area in Ger- New circles. Old circles. man sq. m. 1. Circle of the Isar, 311.14 Upper Bavaria, Lower Danube, Upper Pfalz, Regen, Upper Franconia, 127:35 Upper Main, Middle Franconia, 137.72 Rezat, Lower Franconia 169.54 Lower Main, & Aschaffenburg, Suabia-Neuburg, Upper Danube, 173.95 1394.3 Physical features.] Bavaria is generally a level country, except towards the S, where it is very and the Voralberg, and partakes of the wild and rugged nature of these Alpine regions. On the NE also, towards Bohemia, there is a mountainous frontier, in which several summits reach an alt. of from 3,000 to 4,000 ft.—The B. mountains belong to the two great German chains, the Alps and Sudetes. To that of the Alps belong the Noric Alps, in the circle of the Isar, the Arlberg mountains, the Allgau Alps, the Spessart, the Steigerwald, the Rhöngebirge, the Fichtelgebirge, and the Thuringian forest, of which last, however, only one point, entering the circle of the Upper Main, belongs to B. On the W side of the Rhine, the duchy of Deuxponts is intersected by the chain of the Wasgau or Vosges, which on the side of Germany are steep and rugged, whilst towards France they present a gentle declivity. The defiles and narrow passes of this chain, - particularly the defile of Keyserslautern,-were the scene of many sanguinary and hot contested engagements between the French and Prussians, in the commencement of the revolutionary wars of the last cent.—B. is in general very well-watered. The principal river is the Danube, which enters Bavaria on the SW, about 2 m. S from Ulm, and to the basin of which belong the Iller, the Lech and its affluent the Wertach, the Isar and its affluent the Ammer, the Inn and its affluent the Salzach, all flowing into it on the r. bank; and the Kocher, the Wornitz, the Altmuhl, the Naab, the Regen, and the Ilz on its l. bank. The next river is the Rhine, to the basin of which belong the Lauter, the Queich, the Nahe, and the Blies. The Main, a principal river of the N of Bavaria, also belongs to the basin of the Rhine; its tributary rivers are the Regnitz, the Rodach, the Itz, the Kinzig, the Lohr, and the Tauber. It is navigable above Bamberg. To the basin of the Weser belongs the Fulda; to that of the Elbe, the Eger and Saale both rising in the Fichtelgebirge.—The principal navigable canal in B. is that which unites the Frankenthal with the Rhine. In 1834 a prospectus was issued for the formation of a junction canal betwixt the Danube and the Rhine, by means of the Main and the Altmuhl. This will be a magnificent work when fully executed. Its length will be nearly 107 m. Its breadth is to be 54 ft. at top, and 34 ft. at bottom; with a depth of 5 ft. The emperor Charlemagne contemplated a canal nearly in the same line as this. There is a cut between Worth and Knitlingen; and another between Rosenheim and Kufstein.—The S part of B. abounds in forests, lakes, and morasses. It is calculated that there are no less than 16 large lakes, 160 smaller lakes, 275 rivers and streamlets, and 360 forests, in this country. Besides the lake of Constance—of which only Isar circle; the Wurmsee or Stambergersee, the Kothe Danube, covering about 70 sq. m.; the moss of Erding in the circle of the Isar, said to exceed 100 sq. m.; and the moss of the Isar, which is nearly 30 m. long, and 12 m. broad. These morasses are overgrown with rushes, and only in a few parts present peat.—The most extensive plain occurs between Ratisbon and Osterhofen. Climate, soil, and produce. The climate of B.which lies between the 47th and 51st parallels of N lat. -is on the whole temperate and healthy; the air, however, is sharp in some parts on account of the elevation. In the valleys, along the Danube and the Main, a very mild climate prevails. In the valleys of the Main, the Saale, and the Tauber, the vine is successfully cultivated; and in some parts of the valley of the Main, chestnut and even almond-trees thrive well.—Southern, or Upper Bavaria, though very mountainous, is by no means so unfit for cultivation as has been generally supposed. Amidst the rugged and stupendous mountains which here occur, the eye is frequently relieved by beautiful valleys whose soil is so rich as to repay more than six-fold even the awkward and unskilful culture of a Bavarian farmer. The tract of country which stretches from Munich, along the banks of the Danube and Inn, is the finest arable land in B., and beautifully diversified with hills clothed to their summits with magnificent forests. The upper palatinate, on the N of the Danube, is a connected chain of mountains rising gradually from the Danube to the Fichtelgebirge and the mountains of Bohemia; yet these lands afford excellent pasturage, and are in many cases susceptible of culture. A German journalist informs us that agriculture is so much neglected in B., that, except in good years, it does not produce grain sufficient for its own consumption; this reproach is certainly no longer applicable to Bavaria, which now raises annually nearly 6,000,000 quarters of grain. The wretched state of agriculture in this country might be chiefly ascribed some years ago to the ignorance of the secular and ecclesiastical proprietors, and to a weak administration. A most injudicious and oppressive mode of taxation prevailed, under which no exemption was made in favour of the poorer class of labourers, who were taxed at the same rate as their wealthier neighbours. An income exceeding one-third of the whole revenue of the state was also engrossed by the monks, who enjoyed at the same time immunity from taxation. The present government, has, however, effected a great amelioration on the state of the agriculturists; and in a few years, it is to be hoped, the resources of this valuable country will be fully called into action. About ninetwentieths of the surface are already under cultivation.—This area is thus distributed: Acres. 8,171,520 Arable land, . 2,325,120 Meadow land, Vineyards, gardens, dwellings, out-buildings, &c., 309,120 Woods and forests, 5,376,000 Waters, rivers, and lakes, 420,080 1,596,560 Grazing and other land, . . 18,198,400 The proportions of arable land, forest land, and waste, in 100 parts, in the different circles of the kingdom, are nearly as follows: In tillage Forest Waste. Circles and pasture. land. Average, Fruit is produced in great quantity, and the culture of it goes on increasing, particularly in the circles of Rezat, Lower Main, and Rhine. Wine is one of the principal productions of the Lower Main circle; it is also produced on the banks of the lake of Constance, on the Rhine, and in the Upper Main and Rezat circles. Wood is a staple production; and the forests afford employment to many thousands, the extent of forest-land being 6,500,000 acres, or double that of France. Hops are extensively cultivated in the Upper Danube circle; and flax to a great extent in the tract called the B. forest, which also furnishes large quantities of garden-vegetables, particularly cabbage and turnips. The potato is extensively cultivated in the W parts. "The soil is divided into very equal proportions. In the six old circlesthose of the Rezat, Regen, Upper Main, and Isar, and of the Upper and Lower Danube-there are 2,254,603 estates held by 606,989 proprietors. The same equality of distribution prevails in the circle of the Rhine; of the Lower Main we have no authentic returns. The rare occurrence of large properties is shown by the inconsiderable number of individuals, who, as possessing freeholds rated to the land-tax at the value of 8,000 florins (about £765), are eligible to seats in the legislature; for it appeared at the first election, that exclusively of noblemen and persons holding property in towns, the number of such individuals did not exceed 7,181. The laws are favourable to the subdivision of estates. In the circles of the Isar, Regen, and Lower Danube, we find many comparatively large properties, between 200 to 400 tagwerken (170 and 340 acres) in extent; in these quarters, such subdivisions as are seldom known, on account of the thinness of the population. The state possesses, in landed property and groundrents, to the value of about £20,087,000." [Behlen.] In 1834, of a pop. of 4,246,778, 1,863,864 were engaged in or supported by agriculture. Minerals.] B. is not so rich in minerals as the Austrian or Hanoverian dominions. The duchy of Deuxponts, and the palatinate on the W of the Rhine, produce more minerals than all the other B. dominions put together. Quicksilver to the amount of 290 cwt. is annually raised. The mountains which contain this mineral embrace a district of 10 or 12 leagues in length, from Wolfstein on the Lauter, to Creutznach on the Nahe, and 7 or 8 nersberg, Moschillandsberg, Morsfeld, and Wolfstein, have been wrought for centuries. Gold is found in the sands of the Rhine near Germersheim and Sels; titanium, at Bodenmais. The annual produce of iron is about 60,000 tons, but is not sufficient for lead or graphite are annually procured. Copper-glance is obtained in the mines of Deuxponts; but the whole amount of copper annually produced does not exceed 770 cwt. The salt-springs of Isar and Lower Main produce about 30,000 tons a-year. Manufactures and commerce.] There is as yet little manufacturing industry in B. Even the manufacture of woollen cloth-which in such a climate is one of the neglected. More than a century ago, 7,000 pieces of woollen cloth were annually fabricated in this country, whereas at present it scarcely produces 5,000. The annual export of linen is valued at £12,000. The yearly importation of cotton goods exceeds the exportation. One of the chief articles of export is wood, which is floated down the rivers, and conveyed by the Danube to Hungary; tobacco—which is manufactured throughout the whole of this country— likewise affords considerable traffic. The other articles of exportation are: salt, corn, rough hides, raw wool, butter, cheese, flax and hemp. The bishopric of Bamberg exports a considerable quantity of grain, as also fruits, saffron, and liquorice. Straw-plaiting has increased considerably of late years: ten years ago the annual exportation of this article exceeded £16,000. Glass is exported to the value of £100,000 annually. The principal seats of industry, after Augsburg and Nuremberg, are Ansbach, Bamberg, Furth, Memmingen, Schwanfurt, and Wurtzburg. The porcelain of Nymphenburg, in the vicinity of Munich, is highly esteemed. Nuremberg is celebrated for its brass wares; and the jewellers of Munich and Augsburg are in repute. The optical and philosophical instruments made at Munich are highly esteemed. The national industry has been greatly augmented by the exertions of the government during the last twenty years. Mechanics' schools are extensively organized. The proportion of the jointrevenue of the Zollverein assigned to B., is about 17 per cent. Railroads.] B. has not been backward in appreciating the advantages of railway communication, although its railways at present exist only in project, with the exception of one from Fürth to Nuremberg, and another from Augsburg to Munich. The first, which is 2 Bavarian leagues in length, was constructed by a company of shareholders, and opened in 1835. The dividend declared upon it in January 1842 exceeded 16 per cent. That from Munich to Augsburg, also constructed by shares, is 164 Bavarian leagues in length, and cost 9,015,000 francs, including a portion of the necessary implements. It was opened in October 1840, and the number of passengers conveyed during the year amounted to 253,680, the total receipts for the same period being 547,774 francs. In 1841 a convention was signed between Bavaria and Saxony, for the construction of a railway from Nuremberg to Leipsic, which is to pass through Bavaria, by Erlangen, Bamburg, and Hoff; and through Saxony, by Plauen and Altenburg. The king has also determined on the establishment of a road from Augsburg to Nuremberg, and the entire line from Augsburg to Hoff will be constructed at the expense of the state. The portion which extends from Nuremberg to the frontiers is to be completed in the space of five years. Several other lines are distantly projected: namely, one striking SW from Augsburg into the valley of Nerlach, through Kempten to Lindau, on the lake of Constance; another to Dillingen, thence to join the railway of Neckar and the Rhine; a third will extend to Ulm, pass through Kaustadt, Heilbronn, and Heidelburg, and join Rhenish Bavaria with Bavaria; a fourth, according to arrangements with Saxony, will diverge at Bamburg from the Nuremberg and Hoff line, extend through Coburg and Meiningen, and so reach the Prussian lines. It is probable that a railway will also be established from Munich to Salzburg and Vienna. Population.] B. returned its population to the diet of the German confederacy at 3,500,000 souls; the census of 1833 returned it at 4,187,390, of whom 543,984 belonged to the circle of the Rhine. In 1843, the population was 4,440,327. On the 31st December, 1846, it was returned at 4,504,874. Population. Main c. 8. Suaben-Newburg, or Upper Danube c. The average annual increase of the pop. is about 23,000. This pop.—with the exception of about 4,000 French who live dispersed in the country, and 58,000 Jews-is all of German origin, but of four different tribes. The Bavarians, who are most numerous in the E, are a stout race of men, rude and heavy in their appearance, but, in their general character, patriotic and industrious. The Franks or Franconians, who prevail in the N, are a more refined, lively, and enterprising race. The other two tribes are Rhenish-Germans and Suabians.—The density of the pop. greatly varies in different districts. For example, while in Rhenish Bavaria it is 230 to the sq. m., in the Upper Main it is 171; and in the Isar not quite 100. The number of dwelling-houses in 1826 was 619,482; and the number of families inhabiting them 787,318. Only about one-seventh of the entire pop. reside in towns of any considerable magnitude. In 1825, according to Rudhart, there were 1,384 noble families in B.; and the nobility formed a 900th part of the pop. Religion and Education.] There is no longer any established religion in B.; entire freedom of conscience is allowed, and Catholics and Protestants enjoy equal political rights. The Catholics, however, form the great majority of the population. Von Behlen, in 1828, thus classifies the population of Bavaria according to religious distinctions: Roman Catholics, 2,880,383; Protestants, 1,094,633; Jews, 57,574; other persuasions, 4,427. In 1840 the Catholics were 3,060,694; Protestants, 1,181,216; Jews, 59,288; Reformed church, 2,717; other persuasions, 4,836. According to the concordat of 1817—recognised as the law of the country in 1821—the Catholic archbishops and bishops are named by the king. There are two Catholic archb., viz., those of Munich-et-Fresing and Bamberg; and 6 bishops, viz., Augsburg, Ratisbon, Passau, Eichstadt, Wurtzburg, and Spire. There are 171 Catholic deaneries, and 2,756 parishes. The Protestants are almost all Lutherans. They are governed by a consistory-general and 4 provisional consistories; and have 1,036 parishes. The Protestant clergy are maintained by the state, at an expense of about £28,000 a-year; an annual grant of about £95,000 is made for the support of the inferior Roman Catholic clergy.—The establishments for education are an object of special care with the government, and have made very rapid progress within these few years. Ducpetiaux reckons the proportion of the pop. attending schools in B., in 1831, to have been 1 in 8, being the same proportion as in Scotland in 1834. Public instruction costs the government annually 755,000 florins, or one-sixth of the entire expense of the interior administration. Parochial schools are established in each parish; and at least one-eighth of the pop. receive their education at the public schools. The new university at Munich promises to be one of the most extensive establishments of the kind in Europe. The number of students attending it in 1827 was 1,632; in 1839-40, 1,440; and in 1844-5, 1,360, of whom 118 were foreigners. There are also universities at Landshut, Wurtzburg, and Erlangen; and a Jewish university at Furth. The number of elementary, mechanics, and Sunday schools, was 5,000 in 1821. The royal academy of sciences at Munich, reorganized in 1827, extends its transactions to all the sciences, and the history of B. in particular. There are a number of distinct seminaries for the Catholic priests. Government.] B. was formerly a sovereign monarchy; but by the constitution of 26th May, 1818, a national assembly, divided into two legislative chambers, was recognised. The crown is hereditary in the male line according to primogeniture; but should the male line, with all its agnates, be extinguished, the crown may pass to male descendants in the female line. The king swears at his accession to maintain the constitution, after which he receives the oath of allegiance from his subjects. The present king is of the Catholic faith; but he might be a Protestant, as no law opposes it. No general law can be introduced without the consent of the chambers. The chambers' consent must be obtained in the imposition of taxes; and without their consent no new ones can be imposed. The first chamber consists of the princes of the royal family, the first officers of the crown, the two archbishops, the higher nobility or hereditary senators, one bishop named by the king, the president of the Protestant consistory, and those persons whom the king may name either for life or hereditary members. The present number of members is about 50. The second chamber consists of deputies elected by the landed proprietors, the universities, the cities and boroughs, and the Roman Catholic and Protestant clergy. This chamber is at present composed of about 120 members. Every six years a new election takes place; but the old members can be elected again. The chambers are to be called together at least once in every three years.—The administration is directed by a council, consisting of the king, the princes of the blood, the ministers of state, a fieldmarshal, and six counsellors named by the king. The ministry is divided into five departments. The kingdom is divided into eight circles; at the head of each is a royal commissary, who is president of a councilof-administration. The police is not a peculiar department in B., but is exercised by the council-ofadministration which reports to the minister of the home department. Finance. In 1819 the excess of the expenditure over the income of B. was 2,007,800 florins (about £192,415); the national debt amounted to 105,740,173 florins (£10,133,430); and the surplus fund towards the redemption of this debt was 1,550,000 florins (£148,542). In the same year the financial laws enacted by the legislature, fixed the income for the year at 31,126,811 florins (£2,982,986), and the expenditure at 31,017,596 (£2,972,519). In the year 1831-1832 the revenues produced 29,217,006 florins (£2,799,963), and the expenditure amounted to 27,095,883 florins (£2,596,688). The national debt between the years 1819 and 1829, had increased from £10,133,430 to £11,392,019; and in 1833 it had reached £12,595,276. The nett public income of B. for the financial period, 1832-1837, was fixed by the legislature at £2,738,656; the charges of management, £971,656, having been previously deducted. The expenses of management amount, therefore, to nearly 26\frac{1}{25} per cent. on the gross revenue of £3,710,312. If we assume the pop. to be 4,200,000, the average amount of revenue contributed by each individual will be 13s. 01d. per annum. The expenditure for the same period, with a reserved fund of £52,405, was fixed at a sum exactly corresponding with the income, of which £2,329,518 were appropriated to the general expenditure of the state, and £409,138 to the budgets of the several circles. Among the items of expenditure were, for the royal household and foreign affairs, £48,560; education and civilization, £73,581; public worship, £128,044; public safety, £39,675; the construction of highways, bridges, &c., £118,087; interest and redemption of the national debt, £785,255; civil list, £287,500; army expenses, £546,250, independently of the gendarmerie. The annual revenue for the financial fund from 1837 to 1843 was estimated at 30,012,473 florins, or £2,702,020. Military resources.] Every Bavarian is liable to the conscription after he has completed his twentyfirst year. The period of service is six years. No Bavarian can settle or marry, or receive any definitive appointment, before he has done all that the law requires with regard to his liability to bear arms; but certain exemptions are granted in the case of nobles, ecclesiastics, and students. B. furnishes the largest contingent of any exclusively German state. It forms the seventh corps of the confederate forces, amounting to 35,600 men. The real strength of the army, however, supposing the present scale of its organization to remain, is as follows:- War. Peace. Infantry—16 regts. of the line, each comp. being competent > to serve a battery of 8 cannon,) 2 comp. of sappers, 1 of miners, 1 of) 720 pontoonmen, and 1 of arti- 650 ncers, 53,594 55,224 The landwehr, or militia, is composed of all Bavarians who have not been already drafted into the ranks of the active army, or battalions of reserve, and are not under 19 or above 60 years of age. On the scale projected, it would amount to 250,000 men and upwards, independently of any levies in the Rhenish territory. The expense of the military establishment for the six years, 1825-1831, was 41,719,962 florins, which averages 6,953,327 florins, or £666,366 per annum.—The fortified places in Bavaria are: Landau, the strongest of its fortresses, in the circle of the Rhine; Passau, on the Danube; Würzburg, in conjunction with the citadel of Marienberg; Ingolstadt, at the confluence of the Schütter and Danube, in the circle of Regen; and Vorchheim, in the circle of the Lower Main. B. has a right to a military road through the territory of Baden, which gives direct access to its dominions on the Rhine. Topography.] The eight circles into which B. was divided between 1817 and 1837 bear the names of the principal rivers; in the latter year a new division was made, or rather a new nomenclature substituted.—The chief towns are, in the order of their pop., Munich, Nuremberg, Augsburg, Ratisbon, Wurzburg, Bamberg, Anspach, Furth, Baireuth, Erlangen- Passau, and Schwabach.-In 1837, the kingdom contained 208 towns,—of which 40 had a pop. of upwards of 3,000, and 9 upwards of 9,000; 410 villages; 23,462 hamlets; and 19,962 isolated houses. History.] B. was subjected to the Roman sceptre under the name of Vindelicia and Noricum; and when Theodoric, after having conquered Odoacer, founded a new Ostro-Gothic empire' B. was comprehended in it. After the fall of that monarchy, the Bavarians were conquered by the Frankish king, Theodoric of Astrasia; but they preserved the prerogative of choosing their own chief. About the year 556, the family of the Agilolfinger possessed this dignity, which they held till the end of the 8th cent. Among them was Odilo, son-in-law of Charles Martel, in whose reign, in the middle of the 8th cent., the archb. Bonifacius divided the B. churches into four bishoprics, namely, Salzburg, Passau, Regensburg, and Freysingen. Tharsilo II. was obliged to swear allegiance to Pepin at Compeigne; and having been subsequently accused of rebellion against Charlemagne, was shut up in a monastery. With him the dynasty of the Agilolfingers was extinguished. Charlemagne abolished the dignity of duke of B., and named his brother-in-law, Count Gerold, governor of this province, into which he introduced the Frankish feudal system, and the division into gaus, governed by grafen or counts. B. afterwards fell to the portion of Louis the German; on whose death, in 840, Carlomann, his son, was named king of B. With Louis IV., who died in 911, the Carlovingian race was extinguished, and Arnulph II., son of the Bavarian general, Luitpold, assumed the dignity of duke, and the sovereign power. Under Arnulph's successors B. was torn by perpetual contentions. In 1071, Guelph, son of Azo of Este, became duke of B. In 1138, the dukedom passed to the house of Austria; but in 1154 it returned to the house of Guelph, in the person of Henry the Lion. In 1180, it again returned to the first family, by the accession of Otto of Wittelsbach, a descendant of Arnulph II. In the 15th cent., the present B. family obtained the palatinate by marriage. Frederic V. elector-palatine, who married Elizabeth, daughter of James I. of England, aspired to the crown of Bohemia, but was unsuccessful, and the electorate was transferred to the house of B. In 1685, the duke of Neuburg, a branch of the Bavarian house, succeeded to the palatinate on the death of the last prince without children. This was an unfortunate event for the inhabitants of the palatinate, as the family of Neuburg were staunch Catholics, whereas the princes of the palatine house had always been zealous Protestants. By the death of Emeric Joseph, the last elector of B., without children, Charles Theodore, elector-palatine, of the house of Neuburg, succeeded to the B. dominions; and the two electorates became united in his person, namely those of B. and the Rhenish palatinate. Charles Theodore died in 1797, and was succeeded by his nephew, then duke of Deuxponts, grandfather of the present sovereign, who assumed the government under the name of Maximilian Joseph III., in 1799. He ceded to France, in the peace of Luneville, the prov. on the l. bank of the Rhine, and the districts of the Inn to Austria; but, on the other hand, B. was successively aggrandized by the secularization of the estates of the clergy in 1803,—by acquisitions at the peace of Presburg in 1805,— by the Rhenish confederacy in 1806,—and by the peace of Vienna in 1809. By the peace of Presburg, the royal title was conceded to the elector of B; and by its close connection with France, B. rose to be the first state in the Rhenish confederacy. At the congress of Vienna, the Prussian principalities of Baireuth were conceded to B., but its sovereign was obliged to relinquish the Tyrol and Voralberg to Austria, for which, however, he obtained Wurzburg and Aschaffenburg. Other exchanges again took place; till at last, at the congress held at Frankfort on 20th July, 1819, matters were settled on their present basis. On the 26th of May, 1818, Maximilian granted a constitution to his subjects; and in February, 1819, the first convocation of the States was held at Munich. Maximilian really did much good to his country, in which he was very popular. He died in 1825, and was succeeded by his son Ludwig or Lewis I. who abdicated on the 20th of March 1848 in favour of his son Maximilian, now Maximilian II. Authorities.] Encyclopedie Methodique.—Busching. -Liechtenstein, Hist. Statist. Uebersicht sæmtlicher Provinzen des König. Baiern. Munich, 1823.—Bæmer Geschichte, Geog. und Stat. des Baiarlandes. Munich: 1823. BAV -Coxe's House of Austria. -Stein. -Berghaus. -Ungewitter.

BAVARIA (Lower), an administrative division or prov. of the kingdom of Bavaria, nearly corresponding to the Under or Lower Danube circle in the former division of the kingdom. This circle has a superficial extent of 194.38 German or 4,128 English sq. m.; with a pop. in 1837 of 515,117; in 1846, of 543,709, of whom 515,482 were Catholics, and only 2,020 Protestants. It contained in 1837, 12 towns, 46 boroughs, 2,048 villages, and 7,028 hamlets and isolated farms. It is divided into 3 arrondissements, viz., Passau, Landshut, and Straubing. The country is on the whole mountainous; the highest chain, the Bohemian forest, skirts the N boundaries. The Danube intersects the circle from W to E; and it is also watered by the Salzach, the Isar, the Ilz, and the Inn. The climate S of the Danube is milder than in the N, where in the neighbourhood of the mountains it is often very cold. Agriculture is here conducted more successfully than in the circle of Upper Bavaria, and the rearing of cattle is well-managed. Passau, the chief town, upon a peninsula formed by the junction of the Inn and the Danube, is strongly fortified; but the advantageous situation of this place for commerce is turned to little use. Not far from Passau, about 5 m. below Deckendorf, the Danube has a fall of 150 ft.
